speaker: interviewer
text: Interviewing Nora Danzig. I'd like to ask you whether you have a message you'd like to tell us from your experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: A message? The only message I can give is I hope none of you young people will never have to experience what my age group experienced. Because I think it's probably left a mark on all of us. And I only hope we haven't put too much of a mark on our children. We probably have, but I hope they can stand it. I think, really, there's nothing further I can add to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What mark do you think has it left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I often wonder what harm-- or have we done any harm to our children? I don't know. Perhaps a young person like you can answer that more than I can. I don't know. I, I certainly haven't gone out of my way to, to put any burden of guilt or anything on my daughter. Because obviously, she had nothing to do with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- I have gone back to Germany. And I-- when I look at somebody older than me, I get worried. I think to myself, are you the one? Were you there? The younger ones, I think, we mustn't blame. Because otherwise, we're as bad as, as, as Hitler's people.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've met some-- I think some wonderful Germans in the meantime. Although I'm very-- always very, very wary. We go skiing, my present husband and I. And we meet a lot of German people then. And at one time, I wouldn't-- I mean, they ask me where I come from. I say England.

speaker: interviewee
text: If-- but sometimes by mistake, I've lapsed into German. And they say, why do you speak such good German? And at one time, I would never tell them. I'd say, we have marvelous schools in England. [LAUGHS] But now, I think to myself, if you ask, I will tell you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they are-- they get-- and I can see they get terribly upset. The-- I'm talking about the younger generation, people that are younger than me. And they-- I can see that they really are physically upset by it. And they have often said to me, our parents have never told us anything. We, we didn't know-- we-- what went on. And we have a big burden to carry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we've made some friends who come to visit us a here and, and a have been wonderfully kind to us and absolutely marvelously friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at some point, you wouldn't have told other Germans, or also other English people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, English people I would tell, would tell, yes. But at one time, I wouldn't-- I mean, there was a time when I wouldn't speak about my sister because I couldn't-- I didn't want to talk about her. Now I'm able to. It's a long time ago now. I mean, the pains never go. The-- you know the old-- it's no cliche. The scar remains and always will. That I'm sure of.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you talk a lot about your experience with, with other people, with other--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think we've all stopped discussing it now. And many-- I, I, I have quite a lot of friends who have gone through the, the camps. They won't talk about it at all. My cousin that, that survived, he is so-- I've never felt and heard such bitterness as that man carries. And it's eating him up. I can see it. He, he won't speak German. He won't have anything German in the house. And he said-- he, he lives in New York. And he said to me, if my president would give me a plane with atom bombs on it, I would happily go over there and drop it now and die doing it. He wouldn't even take-- he's been through, through Auschwitz-- he won't, he won't even take money. He won't even take his pension. He is so bitter. And I-- we've been trying to talk to him, too, too, that he should take. He's entitled to it. And we keep saying to him, they're not all-- surely they can't all be bad. There's no-- it's impossible. He said, I don't want to know. He's so bitter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can just tell me please, what is your, present husband-- what is his background? And what is his name, since you haven't mentioned him?

speaker: interviewee
text: My present husband, his name is Harry Leo. He comes from Leipzig. He was also a refugee. But he was lucky enough to come out with almost all his family, nearly, nearly all his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was one-- I think it was his grandmother. She survived. And she was in France during the war. And they couldn't get her over. This was immediately after the war. And eventually, his father wrote to the queen. Must have been the king-- sorry-- the king then. Was it the queen? Was it the queen-- 1945? I'm getting confused.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, he wrote. And we have a-- he has a letter saying, from a lady-in-waiting, that they should apply again. They will get a permit. And they managed to get over after the war-- very elderly lady. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And are you-- and Harry, as I say, comes from Leipzig. His parents were Polish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I think they, they, they were stateless. But they agreed to take Polish nationality. Because I think the Poles were treated not quite as badly at first as the Germans. But anyway, they got out. Or the parents, his sister, aunts, and uncles, many of them got out, and cousins. They're all-- and many are still in London now. And he has one cousin who is one of I think about four sisters. They, they were in France. And they took the parents away to Auschwitz and left the girls. And somehow, these girls survived in France. And one of them is married to a then GI. He was a soldier-- not Jewish. And she's married to this man and lives in America. He was-- his forefathers was one of the Pilgrim fathers and lives in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to add about any period which we haven't mentioned yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really think there's much more to say, except that I have one or two cousins that survived-- survived, but not in, in camp. Only one managed to get out. I have one cousin living in Brussels with-- married with an extended family. I have a cousin in Canada. And I had one cousin here in London who had two children. And then the children went to America-- to Canada-- and are still there. Unfortunately, the cousin died. And I actually have one other cousin here. There was a cousin that survived camp-- had two sisters. One lived. One came here with a baby in her arms, her baby sister. I think this is quite interesting, because I think this is perhaps interesting for this-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] can't think of the-- can't give it the right name, what we're doing. She brought her baby sister to England in her arms. The baby was taken by a Jewish family and adopted. And they cut her off from the-- from her sister completely. This girl is-- girl-- she's a woman, of course, a little bit younger than I am-- married a, a camp survivor and will have nothing to do with any of us, not even her brother who survived camp nor the sister that died only last year. She's cut herself off from everybody. She-- the only thing I can think of that the couple that adopted her brainwashed her not to mix with anybody. She's rejected her past completely, so much so her children didn't even know she was adopted. I've-- We've all tried to get in touch with her. And she won't have anything to do with us. I've managed to meet her once by a devious-- by devious means. And she won't-- I talked to her then. But she will have-- I have never talked to her since. She doesn't want to know. That's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Thank you very much, Mrs. Danzig. Can you please describe this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this photo, the, the first man on the left is my grandfather. And the woman next to him is my grandmother. The other three people I don't know, except that they were called Lowenthal. All these people are called Lowenthal. I don't know how-- if and how they're related.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Who is in this photo, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo is of my parents' marriage. Obviously, the bride is my mother. And next to her is my father. I think it must have been around in the '20s, perhaps 20-- 1925. Next to my father on the right are my grandparents, maternal grandparents. On the left is my grandfather from my-- the father of my father. The others there are various-- there must be the three sisters there. And the children in the front-- the left one is my oldest cousin, who is still alive today in Canada. The others, I don't really know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is in-- I think in Hosbach, where I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a school picture, self-explanatory by the date. I am in the third row, the fourth child from the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken, I think, a very short while before we left for England. So it was taken probably the end of '38. And starting from the left is my sister, Inge, my mother, and it's me. And there's my father on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I shouldn't have done that, should I? Can you please describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken, I imagine, soon after I arrived in England. And on it in the front is me with our-- with a dog that I hated. [LAUGHS] And behind me stands a friend of my mother's and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This photo is of my official guardian on the right, Anne Morris, and her older sister, Sarah Morris.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was it taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I'm afraid I don't know. I think probably '39, I should think, '38, possibly a bit later. I don't know. That's how I remember them when I first came to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where and when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Perranporth, Cornwall, in the Jewish children's home in 1943. I'm in the first row, first left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of the other girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd have to have it closer to me. I remember quite a few of them. And one of them will be arriving tomorrow for a, a sort of a reunion. There's--

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lily Schindler, [? Lea ?] [? Groomer, ?] Esther [? Groomer, ?] Doris [? Goldschmidt, ?] Mrs. [? Groomer, ?] who was our cook. I can't-- in fact, I can't see. I'd have to have it closer to me to see the rest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken after I came back to London in 1946. And I think I'm in the front. My sister is behind me, and Auntie Anne Morris, my guardian, sitting on the bench.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I don't know. I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo is the second children's home, or hostel if you like, that I was in, a [INAUDIBLE] home. And I am third from the right in the back row. And there are several of the girls from the hostel in it. They're pretty well dispersed all over the place-- America, Israel, and so on, Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do. But I can't see them from here. So I can't really tell you. There was-- there was [INAUDIBLE], probably [? Seta ?] Limberger, Rosemary [? Krull, ?] and Mrs. [? Fulke. ?] No, Miss [? Fulke-- ?] sorry. She was our matron, the second from the left. I think that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo is taken of my sister with, I assume, a friend and a donkey in Trinidad. I don't know who the other lady is. But Inge is the one that's standing up. And it was probably taken in the early '50s, I am guessing.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] on that. Are you able to see it? Are you able to see it? Can you please describe this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of my husband and I, Harry Danzig. And it was taken this year, in 1996, at a friend's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is taken this year, Easter time. And at the back on the left is my daughter, Susan. And next to her is me. And the bottom on the left is Adam, the older child, my grandson, and the younger one, Dominic

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please tell us something about this letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this letter was written before I came to England to my parents. And basically, it's agreeing to take me as well as my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it written?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was written in '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this document, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This document is actually my sister's travel document to come to England. I had an identical one. And I've just acquired this recently on, on a visit to America. I-- and if, if, if one sees the reverse of it, which you can't at the moment, there's a red J on it, J for Jew, Jude.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this the back of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the back of it, yeah-- kinderausweis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please describe to us this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This document is just to say-- as it's in English, you can probably read it-- is to say that Miss Anne Morris, my legal guardian, agrees to take me as, as well as-- well, actually doesn't-- sorry. It doesn't mention my sister-- agrees to take me to England under the auspices of the Hampstead Garden Suburb Care Committee for Refugee Children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please describe this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the [GERMAN] allowing me to leave Germany, which is a deregistration form to say that I may, I may leave. And it-- because that-- I'm sure they haven't got.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please describe this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a list of what was in my suitcase, which we all had to have. The things that are underlined, we were-- obviously were confiscated, even tiny little things like that, which I'm sure had very little value.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what is in the list? Can--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they're basically just clothing. And the red thing-- the, the things that are crossed out were a little necklace, and a watch, and such like, practically without value. But they still took them from us, as it's written underneath, which I can't really read very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this issued at the border, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I, I think we had to have this with us. I, I imagine my father did this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please tell us about this newspaper?

speaker: interviewee
text: This paper is sentencing Dr. Singh to death for the murder of his wife, who is my sister, Inge Paula Braunschweiger. There's a small picture of her on the left. And the man getting in the car is Dr. Singh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please tell us the story of this pendant?

speaker: interviewee
text: This pendant, which, as you can probably tell, has no value except to me, was given by my mother in Theresienstadt to a soldier from the village that I come from who was on duty there. And he returned to Hosbach for leave. And when he came back, they had disappeared. And he-- it was kept for me till after the war when it was given to me by his family. I believe the man is now dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet the man?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I ever met the man. Sorry. I never actually met the man. The only other piece of information which I should have mentioned perhaps, which is perhaps relevant to the, to the story, is my mother, I don't think, died in the gas chamber. She was put into the camp [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] chemist's shop or whatever you like to call it and was found dead there. So I hope that she took her own life to save her going to the gas chamber. I only learned this about two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hosbach, there's a family that know-- well, they-- everybody knows the, the family. I mean, when I walked into Hosbach the first time and asked for the Braunschweiger house, they knew immediately where it was. And it's such a small village that everybody knows everybody. And the soldier, I think, found this out and-- or somebody-- I'm not sure who-- told this story to a woman that is in touch with me who was at school with me.
